summary: Martin Fowler reviews his Magellan Meridian Gold GPS device, received as a Christmas gift. While he finds it an interesting toy rather than a necessity, it has proven most useful for pre-planned bicycle trips around New England. The device excels at showing current location but struggles with on-the-fly route planning due to its small screen. A recent DirectRoute software update adds automatic routing capabilities, but with significant limitations including inability to set multi-point routes and questionable re-routing logic.
key_points: GPS devices are better at showing where you are than telling you where to go without pre-programmed routes
The small screen size makes on-device route planning impractical compared to traditional fold-out maps
Pre-planning routes on a computer and downloading them to the device works well for cycling trips
The DirectRoute software adds useful auto-routing but lacks multi-point routes and road avoidance features
GPS accuracy is generally excellent, with only occasional bizarre location errors
money_quotes: In general the GPS has been good for finding where you are, but not good at telling you where to go - unless you download a route beforehand.
Nothing beats the resolution and screen size of a fold out map.
About once a year I've run into strangeness - such as when biking through Duxbury I looked down at the unit and it told me I was in Estonia.
Massachusetts doesn't believe in road signs.
When we asked for the re-route it instructed us to get off 128 at an exit we'd just passed, then get back on 128 in the same direction.
key_insight: Early GPS devices were useful supplements to traditional navigation methods but lacked the flexibility and intelligence to replace them entirely.
